message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Applying recent advances in medical instruments, information technology,
                        and unprecedented data sharing into COVID-19 research revolutionized medical
                        sciences, and causes some unprecedented analyses, discussions, and
                        models.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Modeling of this dependency is done using four classes of copulas:
                        Clayton, Frank, Gumbel, and FGM. The estimation of the parameters of the
                        copulas is obtained using the maximum likelihood method. To evaluate the
                        goodness of fit of the copulas, we calculate AIC. All computations are
                        conducted on Matlab R2015b, R 4.0.3, Maple 2018a, and EasyFit 5.6, and the
                        plots are created on software Matlab R2015b and R 4.0.3.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>As time passes, the number of tests increases, and the positivity rate
                        becomes lower. The epidemic peaks are occasions that violate the stated
                        general rule –due to the early growth of the number of tests. If we divide
                        data of each country into peaks and otherwise, about both of them, the
                        rising number of tests is accompanied by decreasing the positivity
                        rate.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The positivity rate can be considered a representative of the level of
                        the spreading. Approaching zero positivity rate is a good criterion to scale
                        the success of a health care system in fighting against an epidemic. We
                        expect that if the number of tests is great enough, the positivity rate does
                        not depend on the number of tests. Accordingly, the number and accuracy of
                        tests can play a vital role in the quality level of epidemic data.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key messages</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="simple">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>In a country, increasing the positivity rate is more
                                representative than increasing the number of tests to warn about an
                                epidemic peak.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>Approaching zero positivity rate is a good criterion to scale
                                the success of a health care system in fighting against an
                                epidemic.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>Except for the first half of the epidemic peaks, in a country,
                                the higher number of tests is associated with a lower positivity
                                rate.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:label>-</jats:label>
                      <jats:p>In countries with high test per million, there is no significant
                                dependency between the number of tests and positivity rate.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title:
Analysis of the Number of Tests, the Positivity Rate, and Their Dependency
                    Structure during COVID-19 Pandemic

reference.article-title: Mathematical modeling the epicenters of coronavirus disease-2019 (COVID-19) pandemic
